research.product: Microsoft Graph OneDrive/SharePoint large-file upload sessions
research.interface: Microsoft Graph REST API v1.0, driveItem: createUploadSession + uploadSession PUT protocol

official_sources.title: driveItem: createUploadSession - Microsoft Graph v1.0
official_sources.used_for: POST /drives/{driveId}/items/{parentItemId}:/{fileName}:/createUploadSession request with item.@microsoft.graph.conflictBehavior body; uploadUrl/expirationDateTime response; fragment PUTs with Content-Length and Content-Range 'bytes {first}-{last}/{total}'; sequential order requirement; 320 KiB (327,680-byte) fragment multiple rule and sub-60 MiB request cap; 202 + nextExpectedRanges intermediate responses; final 200/201 with the driveItem; 416 on already-received ranges with status query recovery; GET uploadUrl to resume; 404 = session gone, restart the entire upload; 5xx retry-with-backoff best practice; no Authorization header on the preauthenticated uploadUrl; DELETE to cancel

official_sources.title: uploadSession resource type - Microsoft Graph v1.0
official_sources.used_for: uploadSession property semantics: uploadUrl accepts the byte-range PUTs; expirationDateTime is UTC and extends with every fragment; nextExpectedRanges is a collection of zero-indexed 'start-end'/'start-' strings listing ranges the server is still missing

research.language_rationale: The uploader extends an existing Java asset-sync service that already speaks Graph REST through java.net.http; the upload-session protocol is plain HTTP with exact header semantics (Content-Range, no-auth PUTs), which the stdlib HttpClient pins more faithfully than the Graph Java SDK's LargeFileUploadTask abstraction.

verified_facts: An upload session is created with POST .../items/{parentItemId}:/{fileName}:/createUploadSession carrying Bearer auth and an optional JSON body whose item.@microsoft.graph.conflictBehavior is fail (default) / replace / rename; the item name must match the path segment.
The response is an uploadSession with uploadUrl (preauthenticated), expirationDateTime (UTC, extended by every fragment), and optionally nextExpectedRanges.
File bytes are PUT to uploadUrl with Content-Length and Content-Range 'bytes {first}-{last}/{total}'; the declared total must be identical across fragments; fragments go up sequentially.
Fragment sizes must be multiples of 320 KiB (327,680 bytes) except the final fragment, and each request must stay under 60 MiB, otherwise commits can fail.
Intermediate fragments return 202 Accepted with nextExpectedRanges such as ["26-"]; the final fragment returns 200 OK or 201 Created with the completed driveItem (id, name, size, file facet).
Do not send the Authorization header on the PUTs to uploadUrl — only the initial POST uses the bearer token; including it can yield 401.
A PUT for bytes the server already received returns 416 Requested Range Not Satisfiable; the documented recovery is to GET the uploadUrl for status and resume from the reported nextExpectedRanges.

GET on the uploadUrl returns the current expirationDateTime plus nextExpectedRanges; entries are zero-indexed 'start-end' or open-ended 'start-' strings, may omit some missing ranges, and are not a fragment-size template.
404 on the uploadUrl means the session no longer exists; the documented handling is to start the entire upload over with a fresh session.
5xx responses during fragment upload should be retried with exponential backoff; DELETE on the uploadUrl cancels the session (204).
